original_hypothesis.title: ProfitSharingConfig Precision Loss from uint32 Truncation Causes Systematic Profit Misdistribution
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: ProfitManager.setProfitSharingConfig() validates that splits sum to 1e18 at 18-decimal precision, then stores them as uint32 values after dividing by 1e9. This truncation loses up to (1e9 - 1) per split. When splits are read back via getProfitSharingConfig(), they're multiplied by 1e9, and creditSplit is computed as the residual (1e18 - surplusBufferSplit - guildSplit - otherSplit). The truncation means the actual distribution differs from configured intent, with the residual creditSplit absorbing the error. Over many distributions, this systematic error compounds.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: This is a demonstrable precision error in the storage packing optimization. While individual impact per distribution is small, it represents incorrect code that accumulates over the protocol's lifetime.

proof_of_signal.pattern: uint32(surplusBufferSplit / 1e9) truncates; configured splits summing to 1e18 may not reconstruct accurately after storage round-trip

preconditions: setProfitSharingConfig is called with values not evenly divisible by 1e9

initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Governor configures splits with values not aligned to 1e9 boundary
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: creditSplit absorbs truncation error; up to ~3e9 total rounding error across 3 stored splits per distribution

economic_questions: What is the maximum per-distribution misdistribution in absolute CREDIT terms?
Does the error systematically favor credit holders (residual absorbs positive truncation)?
Over protocol lifetime, what is the cumulative impact?
validation_requirements: Set splits with values not divisible by 1e9, read back, compute difference
Calculate amountForGuild and amountForCredit with truncated vs intended splits

preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Up to ~3 gwei per split per distribution cycle (requires validation)
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_profit_margin: Negligible per transaction, cumulative over time

market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Minimal per-event, cumulative over protocol lifetime

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: GUILD voters or surplus buffer (whichever loses from truncation)

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Check actual configured split values vs 1e9 divisibility
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Compute exact truncation error for realistic split configurations

validation_result.mechanism: uint32 truncation in profit sharing config storage
validation_result.impact_summary: setProfitSharingConfig validates splits sum to 1e18 (18 decimals) but stores 3 of 4 splits as uint32 after dividing by 1e9, truncating up to (1e9-1) per split. creditSplit absorbs the accumulated truncation error as a residual, systematically misdistributing profits compared to the configured intent.
validation_result.detailed_analysis: The defect occurs in the storage packing of ProfitSharingConfig. The setter validates `surplusBufferSplit + otherSplit + guildSplit + creditSplit == 1e18` at 18-decimal precision, then stores surplusBufferSplit, guildSplit, and otherSplit as `uint32(value / 1e9)`. Integer division truncates any sub-1e9 remainder. creditSplit is never stored; it is recomputed as `1e18 - (stored values * 1e9)` in both getProfitSharingConfig() and effectively in notifyPnL() where amountForCredit is computed as the residual. This means: (1) surplusBufferSplit, guildSplit, and otherSplit each lose up to (1e9-1) precision, (2) creditSplit absorbs the sum of all truncation errors, gaining up to ~3*(1e9-1) ≈ 3e9 wei per distribution. The effective distribution differs from what the governor configured. For example, if governor sets all four splits to ~0.25e18 with sub-1e9 fractional parts, the actual credit split could be ~3e9 wei higher than intended per distribution.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: The defect is clearly present in the code. setProfitSharingConfig validates at 18-decimal precision but truncates to 9-decimal precision during storage. There is no requirement that input values be divisible by 1e9, no documentation of the truncation behavior, and no rounding (only floor truncation via integer division). The notifyPnL function reads the truncated values and computes amountForCredit as a residual, confirming the credit split absorbs the error.

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: Validation requires exact sum at 18-decimal precision, but storage truncates 3 splits to 9-decimal precision via integer division. The 4th split (creditSplit) is not stored and is later recomputed as a residual, absorbing the accumulated truncation error from the other 3 splits.

Uses the truncated uint32 values directly. amountForCredit is computed as a residual, absorbing the truncation error. Each of the three stored splits may be lower than intended, causing amountForCredit to be systematically higher than the configured creditSplit.

Reconstructed values differ from original inputs. surplusBufferSplit, guildSplit, and otherSplit are rounded down to nearest 1e9 boundary. creditSplit absorbs the positive remainder, so sum still equals 1e18 but individual splits differ from what was configured.

calling_sequence: Governor calls setProfitSharingConfig with splits summing to 1e18 (e.g. surplusBufferSplit=333333333333333334, creditSplit=333333333333333333, guildSplit=333333333333333333, otherSplit=0)
Storage: surplusBufferSplit stored as uint32(333333333333333334/1e9) = uint32(333333333), guildSplit stored as uint32(333333333)
LendingTerm calls notifyPnL with profit amount
notifyPnL reads profitSharingConfig from storage (truncated values)
amountForCredit = amount - amountForSurplusBuffer - amountForGuild (slightly more than intended 1/3)
execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: The truncation occurs at setProfitSharingConfig where `uint32(surplusBufferSplit / 1e9)` drops sub-1e9 precision. This propagates to every notifyPnL call where the truncated splits are used for distribution. The creditSplit, being computed as a residual `amount - other_amounts`, systematically absorbs the positive error.

config_validation.deployment_evidence: No deployment scripts provided that set specific profit sharing config values. The defect exists regardless of specific configuration as long as any split is not evenly divisible by 1e9.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: This is not an attack scenario but a design defect. Governor configures profit splits with 18-decimal precision (e.g., surplusBufferSplit=151111111111111100). The code truncates to uint32(151111111) during storage, losing 111111100 of precision. When profits are distributed, surplusBuffer and guild receive slightly less than intended, while credit holders receive the excess. The maximum misdistribution is ~3e9 wei (~3 gwei) per distribution event across all three stored splits. Over the protocol's lifetime, this accumulates but remains negligible in absolute terms.
validation_result.impact_assessment: The impact per distribution is at most ~3 gwei (3e9 wei) of misdistribution, which is economically negligible. However, this constitutes a genuine code defect: the code validates splits at 18-decimal precision but stores at 9-decimal precision without requiring inputs to be 1e9-aligned or documenting the truncation. The credit holders systematically receive slightly more than their configured share at the expense of surplus buffer, guild holders, and/or other recipient. The discrepancy between configured and actual splits could mislead governance.
recommended_mitigations: Add a requirement that input splits are divisible by 1e9: require(surplusBufferSplit % 1e9 == 0 && guildSplit % 1e9 == 0 && otherSplit % 1e9 == 0 && creditSplit % 1e9 == 0, 'splits must be 1e9-aligned')

Alternatively, validate the sum at 9-decimal precision instead of 18: require(surplusBufferSplit/1e9 + otherSplit/1e9 + guildSplit/1e9 + creditSplit/1e9 == 1e9) and document that sub-1e9 precision is lost
Alternatively, use uint64 for storage (still fits in a single slot with address) to support higher precision
validation_result.replication_plan: 1. Deploy ProfitManager with all dependencies. 2. Call setProfitSharingConfig(333333333333333334, 333333333333333333, 333333333333333333, 0, address(0)). 3. Call getProfitSharingConfig() and observe creditSplit = 334000000000000000 instead of expected 333333333333333333. 4. Trigger notifyPnL with a profit of 1e18. 5. Verify amountForCredit exceeds the intended 333333333333333333.
uncertainties: Whether the governor would ever set splits not divisible by 1e9 in practice (depends on governance tooling and UI)
Whether this was an intentional design trade-off that was simply not documented
additional_testing_needed: Verify with a concrete Foundry test that demonstrates the truncation and misdistribution
Check GIP_0 or other governance proposals to see what actual split values are used in deployment
